trans-unit.source: Why Does the Battery Charge and Discharge at the Same Electricity Price?

The <ph class="- topic/ph " conref="en-us_topic_0000001645659009.xml#ph122067484595" id="ph1959151418587" idp:conrefpath="200650494000#EN-US_BOOKMAP_0000001596019118#EN-US_TOPIC_0000001645659009" idp:userph="true"/> uses AI algorithms to forecast future PV output, load demand, and grid status.

If a sharp increase in load or a sudden decrease in PV output is forecast, the system will charge in advance to build up a power reserve, even if electricity prices remain the same, to cope with short-term power deficits.

For example, when the system forecasts that high-power appliances (such as an air conditioner or electric water heater) will be turned on in the home one hour later and PV output may be insufficient at that time, it will charge from the grid in advance to ensure sufficient energy to cope with the peak load.

The <ph class="- topic/ph " conref="en-us_topic_0000001645659009.xml#ph122067484595" id="ph16288346152013" idp:conrefpath="200650494000#EN-US_BOOKMAP_0000001596019118#EN-US_TOPIC_0000001645659009" idp:userph="true"/> comprehensively considers electricity prices and loads over a longer time horizon.

If the system forecasts that the electricity price peak will be higher the next day, it may choose to charge during the current same-price period and reserve energy for a discharge opportunity with higher returns the next day, rather than discharging fully right away.
